result:
The fifth section of the Federal Rules alphabetically on the Cornell Law School's Legal Information Institute (LII) website is the Federal Rules of Evidence.[1](https://www.govinfo.gov/content/pkg/USCODE-2024-title28/pdf/USCODE-2024-title28-app-federalru-dup2-rule601.pdf), [2](https://www.law.cornell.edu/rules/fre/rule_601)

Within the Federal Rules of Evidence, Article VI is titled "Witnesses" and contains the most rules with "witnesses" in their titles, such as "Rule 601. Competency to Testify in General," "Rule 602. Need for Personal Knowledge," "Rule 605. Judge's Competency as a Witness," and "Rule 606. Juror's Competency as a Witness."[3](https://statecodesfiles.justia.com/us/2023/title-28/appendix/federal-rules-of-evidence/content-1292/rule-601/rule-601.pdf?ts=1741093919)

The first rule in Article VI is Rule 601, titled "Competency to Testify in General".[3](https://statecodesfiles.justia.com/us/2023/title-28/appendix/federal-rules-of-evidence/content-1292/rule-601/rule-601.pdf?ts=1741093919)

As of 2021, the last amendment to Federal Rule of Evidence 601 was effective on December 1, 2011. This amendment was part of a general restyling of the Evidence Rules "to make them more easily understood and to make style and terminology consistent throughout the rules."[4](https://www.supremecourt.gov/orders/courtorders/frev11.pdf) These changes were intended to be stylistic only, without altering the substantive meaning of the rule.

Comparing the language of Rule 601 before and after the 2011 restyling:
*   The original proposed text, as described in the Committee Notes, stated: "Every person is competent to be a witness except as otherwise provided in these rules."[4](https://www.supremecourt.gov/orders/courtorders/frev11.pdf)
*   The restyled text, effective December 1, 2011, and current as of 2021, states: "Every person is competent to be a witness unless these rules provide otherwise. But in a civil case, state law governs the witness's competency regarding a claim or defense for which state law supplies the rule of decision."[4](https://www.supremecourt.gov/orders/courtorders/frev11.pdf)

In the process of restyling the first clause from "except as otherwise provided in these rules" to "unless these rules provide otherwise," the word "in" was deleted.
